policy_statement: Electrify 10% of total existing commercial buildings by 2035.
mechanism_description: Electrifying existing commercial buildings replaces on-site fossil fuel use with electricity, reducing direct building energy-related greenhouse gas emissions.
secondary_justification: Resource Efficiency: Electrification commonly enables higher end-use efficiency (e.g., heat pumps deliver more useful heat per unit of delivered energy than combustion), reducing energy input for the same building service and thus lowering fuel/energy throughput.
primary_causal_pathway: Building energy service demand → fuel switching (fossil → electricity) → ↓ on-site combustion + (often) ↓ lifecycle GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric GHG concentration growth
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change: Commercial buildings replace fossil-based equipment (gas boilers/furnaces, gas water heaters, etc.) with electric alternatives (heat pumps, electric resistance/induction where applicable).  
(2) Mediating process: Reduced direct combustion of methane/oil eliminates associated CO₂ (and reduces upstream methane leakage exposure), and efficient electric technologies can reduce total final energy needed for the same heating/service.  
(3) Outcome: Lower building-related GHG emissions (immediate Scope 1 reductions; broader reductions depending on grid emissions intensity over time).
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced greenhouse gas emissions from commercial building energy use by replacing fossil fuel combustion with electrified end-use technologies.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: The core causal chain is fuel switching in commercial buildings that reduces GHG emissions from building energy use.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While electrification can improve efficiency and indoor air quality, the primary climate intent and largest climate effect is emissions mitigation via eliminating on-site fossil combustion (and enabling cleaner energy supply over time).  
3) Inherent causal chain for each secondary:  
   - Resource Efficiency: Many electrification measures intrinsically raise conversion efficiency for key loads (notably heating via heat pumps), so the same service typically requires less delivered energy.  
4) Edge cases applied: If electricity is supplied by a highly fossil-intensive grid, near-term net GHG reductions can be smaller or uncertain, but the mechanism’s dominant objective and typical effect remains emissions reduction through fuel switching and compatibility with grid decarbonization.  
5) Confirm labels apply to ALL instances: Any policy that electrifies commercial buildings (as defined by this mechanism) is fundamentally a mitigation action; efficiency gains are a common, mechanism-linked secondary effect, though magnitude varies.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Net GHG impact depends on marginal grid emissions, refrigerant leakage from some HVAC equipment, and whether electrification is paired with efficiency/weatherization; however, the mechanism remains principally mitigation via eliminating on-site fossil combustion in commercial buildings.
co_benefits: Improved indoor air quality (reduced combustion pollutants); potential operating cost savings and reduced exposure to gas price volatility.
instance_edge_case_notes: Could be implemented via mandates or incentives in practice, but the text itself only specifies an outcome target (no compliance mechanism or funding specified), so it is classified as target-setting and indirect.
